era_context: The early 16th century saw the height of the Portuguese maritime empire under Manuel I, the expansion of the Ottoman Empire under Selim I, the Italian Renaissance in full flourish, and the early stages of the Protestant Reformation's intellectual ferment (though Luther's 95 Theses would not be posted until 1517). In Asia, the Ming Dynasty ruled China, the Mughal Empire was consolidating power in India under Babur, and Southeast Asian kingdoms competed for regional dominance. The Americas remained unknown to Europeans, though indigenous civilisations—the Aztec Empire and Inca Empire—were at their territorial peaks.

events.title: Portuguese conquest of Malacca
events.description: Afonso de Albuquerque led a Portuguese fleet to capture the strategic port city of Malacca from the Malay Sultanate. The conquest was brutal, involving naval bombardment and assault. Malacca became the cornerstone of Portuguese control over the Strait of Malacca and the spice trade routes to the Indian Ocean and beyond. This victory established Portuguese dominance in Southeast Asian maritime commerce for the next century and marked a turning point in European colonial expansion into Asia.

sources.name: Commentários do Grande Afonso Dalboquerque by Afonso de Albuquerque (compiled by his son, early 16th century)
Suma Oriental by Tomé Pires (Portuguese merchant, early 16th century)
Historiographic consensus from Portuguese colonial records and modern scholarship

events.certainty_note: The conquest is well-documented in Portuguese sources and confirmed by multiple contemporary accounts. The date of August 1511 is firmly established.

events.title: Selim I becomes Ottoman Sultan
events.description: Selim I ascended to the Ottoman throne following the abdication of his father Bayezid II. Selim's reign marked a dramatic shift toward aggressive military expansion and centralization. He would become known as 'Selim the Grim' for his ruthless consolidation of power, elimination of rivals, and military campaigns. His reign saw the Ottoman Empire expand significantly into the Middle East and North Africa, fundamentally reshaping the geopolitical balance of the Mediterranean and Eastern Mediterranean regions.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and Defter-i Muhasebe (Ottoman administrative documents)

events.certainty_note: The accession date is well-documented in Ottoman administrative records. April 1512 is sometimes cited, but 1511 is the traditional date of Bayezid II's abdication.

events.title: Establishment of Portuguese trading post in Goa
events.description: Following the conquest of Malacca, Afonso de Albuquerque turned his attention to Goa on the Indian coast. Portuguese forces attacked and captured Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate in November 1510, but the city was retaken by local forces in 1511. Albuquerque recaptured it definitively in December 1511. Goa became the capital of the Portuguese Estado da Índia and remained the center of Portuguese power in Asia for over four centuries, serving as a crucial hub for the spice trade and Christian missionary activity.
events.certainty_note: The recapture of Goa in December 1511 is well-documented in Portuguese sources. The initial capture in 1510 and loss in 1511 are both confirmed.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's reign continues in Ming China
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521) was in the middle of his reign, known for his unconventional behavior and delegation of power to eunuchs, particularly Liu Jin. The emperor's disinterest in traditional governance led to significant eunuch influence over state affairs. This period saw continued economic prosperity but also growing institutional instability. The Zhengde era is remembered as one of the more chaotic periods of Ming rule, though cultural and artistic achievements continued.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Zhengde Emperor's reign dates and the influence of eunuchs are well-documented in Ming administrative records.

events.title: Babur consolidates Mughal power in northern India
events.description: Babur, founder of the Mughal Empire, was consolidating his control over northern India following his victory at the First Battle of Panipat in 1526 (note: this entry refers to his earlier campaigns in 1511). Babur was engaged in campaigns to expand his territory and eliminate rival sultanates. His military innovations, including the use of gunpowder artillery and cavalry tactics, were beginning to reshape warfare in the subcontinent. By 1511, Babur had established himself as a major regional power, though his greatest victories lay ahead.

sources.name: Baburnama (Babur's autobiography, written in Chagatai Turkish)

events.certainty_note: Babur's campaigns in this period are documented in his autobiography, though specific events in 1511 are less precisely dated than later campaigns.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Selim I's accession is sometimes disputed: some sources place it in April 1512 rather than 1511, though the abdication of Bayezid II occurred in 1511. The significance of Goa's capture is sometimes overstated in Western historiography—while important for Portuguese trade, it did not immediately disrupt Indian Ocean commerce as dramatically as some accounts suggest. The Malacca conquest, while militarily significant, did not immediately eliminate Arab and Indian merchant networks in Southeast Asia, which continued to operate alongside Portuguese control.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1511 is rich, particularly for European and Ottoman sources. Portuguese colonial records are abundant and detailed. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are comprehensive. However, sources from the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and Africa are less abundant in European archives and may be underrepresented. The perspective is heavily weighted toward Portuguese, Ottoman, and Chinese court records. Indigenous perspectives from conquered regions are largely absent. Modern scholarship has increasingly questioned the 'triumphalist' narrative of Portuguese conquest, noting the resilience and adaptation of local trading networks.

graph_edges.note: Both events represent Portuguese expansion in Asia under Albuquerque in the same year
Selim I's accession led to aggressive Ottoman expansion campaigns
Both are part of the Portuguese Estado da Índia expansion
